pages.text: Effect of matrix strength on pullout behavior of steel ﬁber reinforced very-high strength concrete composites Taher Abu-Lebdeh a,*, Sameer Hamoush a, William Heard b, Brian Zornig c a Department of Civil, Architectural, and Environmental Engineering, North Carolina A&T State University, Greensboro, NC 27411, United States b US Army (ERDC-GSL), Survivability Engineering Branch, 3909 Halls Ferry Road Vicksburg, MS 39180-6199, United States c Manhattan Construction Company, 2120 Montrose, Houston, TX 77006, United States article info Article history: Received 4 February 2010 Received in revised form 12 May 2010 Accepted 19 June 2010 Available online 16 July 2010 Keywords: Very-high strength concrete Fibers–matrix bond Matrix strength Interface Single-ﬁber test Pullout energy abstract This paper presents the results of single-ﬁber pullout tests for deformed and smooth steel ﬁbers embed- ded in the newly developed very-high strength concrete (VHSC) matrixes. The pullout test program involved four types of steel ﬁbers, eight compressive strengths of VHSC matrixes, and two normal con- crete strengths. Test results have shown that pullout behavior of different steel ﬁber reinforced VHSC composites is inﬂuenced by the matrix strength and ﬁber end condition (smooth, ﬂat end, or hooked). Results reveal that both maximum pull-out load and total pullout energy increases as matrix strength increases for all deformed ﬁbers that did not rupture. The test results also indicated that the increase in total pullout energy is more signiﬁcant than that in peak load. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Very-high strength concrete (VHSC) is made from the same gen- eral components as conventional concrete; however, by carefully selecting, proportioning, and mixing the components, signiﬁcant improvements in compressive strength, tensile strength, and toughness can be achieved [1–4]. VHSC is produced using the par- ticle packing theory (selecting materials that are close in volume) which limits the number and size of void spaces, thus reducing the number of possible ﬂaws in the matrix. The addition of ﬁbers to a cementitious matrix greatly improves its toughness. The ﬁbers cross the paths of potential cracks and transmit stress between the ﬁbers and the matrix through the interfacial bond. The ﬁber/matrix bond behavior is commonly as- sessed using the single-ﬁber pullout test. Theoretically, two differ- ent approaches have been used in the past to identify bond parameters: the stress approach which expresses growth of the debonded interface in terms of interfacial stress; and the fracture mechanical approach which expresses interfacial debonding in terms of energy equilibrium. Stang et al. [5] investigated these two approaches. Numerous investigations have been conducted to determine ﬁ- ber–matrix interfacial properties. Naaman and Najm [6] performed a comprehensive experimental program on steel ﬁber pullout tests. Their study includes three different types of steel ﬁbers (smooth, deformed, and hooked) embedded in three different nor- mal strength mortar matrixes with low (33.5 MPa), medium (51.0 MPa) and high (59.7 MPa) strengths. They noticed that de- formed ﬁbers resisted pullout in an oscillatory fashion, while hooked ﬁber resistance consistently decreased as the hook straightened as it traveled through the tunnel. Shannag et al. [7] investigated the interfacial properties of high strength cement based matrix called Densiﬁed Small Par

Although ﬁber–matrix interfacial research has been a popular topic for many years, very-high strength concrete (VHSC) has been developed recently, and has not yet been fully characterized. In this research, a series of pullout tests were performed using VHSC as the medium from which the ﬁbers were pulled. Concrete strength, ﬁber deformation (smooth, hooked, ﬂat end and helical), and four different types of steel ﬁbers were investigated. The pull-out loads versus slips, peak loads, and pullout works (or dissipated bond en- ergy) were evaluated. The main objective of this research is to experimentally investigate the bond mechanisms in VHSC devel- oped by the US Army Corps of Engineers. The results of this exper- imental investigation are important to better understand the effects of ﬁber geometry, matrix properties, and loading have on bond-slip characteristics, and further to improve the tensile and toughness properties of VHSC. 2. Experimental program 2.1. Specimens The overall research program studied the effects of four types of steel ﬁbers, ﬁ- ber embedment lengths, concrete strengths, mechanical deformation, pullout rate and ﬁber embedment/placement methods. A comprehensive and large database was generated for the purpose of characterizing VHSC. This paper focuses on the ef- fect of four types of steel ﬁbers embedded in eight different matrixes of very-high strength concrete and two normal strength concretes. Eighty-eight specimens were prepared to carry out the above overall experi- mental program. Of which, 21 specimens were especially designed to investigate the effect of very-high strength matrixes on the pullout behavior of four types of steel ﬁbers. Each specimen consisted of six embedded ﬁbers, producing six sin- gle-ﬁber pullout tests. The steel ﬁbers used in this investigation include: Dramix 80/30 high carbon ﬁber; Dramix ZP 305 low carbon ﬁber; Fibercon; and Polytorx. Deformed, hooked, and smooth ﬁbers were tested. The smooth ﬁbers were prepared by clipping off the end of the hooked and ﬂattened end ﬁbers. Properties of theses ﬁbers are tabulated in Table 1 . The medium was very-high strength concrete (VHSC) developed by the US Army Corps of Engineer’s. The mix proportions for this VHSC were: 1.00 cement: 0.97 sand: 0.28 silica ﬂour: 0.39 silica fume: 0.0206 high range water reducing admixture (HRWRA): 0.22 water. The speciﬁc gravity was 3.15, 2.65, 2.65, 2.22, 1.3, and 1.0 respectively. The components were dry-mixed at a low rate for 10 min, and then the water/HRWRA were slowly added to the mix. During casting, the specimens were vibrated for several minutes until the frequency of surfacing air bubbles signiﬁcantly diminished. It should be noted that, for each pullout specimen, three 50.8 mm cube specimens were also prepared for compression test. The molds used for the 50.8 mm cubes were solid brass, and as with the specimen mold, the cube molds were vibrated during casting. After casting and adequately vibrating the specimens, they were placed in plastic bags with wet burlap for 36 h. Then, the specimens were removed from their molds and placed in a lime saturated water curing tank for 7 days at room temperature (23 ± 2 /C176C). After the 7 days curing, the VHSC specimens and cubes were placed in a water ﬁlled tank which was placed in an oven set at 90 /C176C for 4 days. After 4 days in the container, they were removed from the water ﬁlled tank and returned to the oven at 90 /C176C for an additional 2 days. Table 2 sum

load ( Pcrit) or point on the initial incline where nonlinear behavior begins is well before the peak load is reached. The post-peak behavior for the hooked (D) ﬁber does not appear to be inﬂuenced by matrix strength. The effects of matrix compressive strength on smooth Dramix ﬁbers are listed in Table 3, and illustrated in Fig. 2b. The peak load of the ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC is 16.8% higher than that of the ﬁbers pulled from the grout. Interestingly, the total pullout work, displacement at the peak and the pullout work at the peak are all higher for the ﬁber pulled from the grout. Fig. 2 b shows that the initial incline of the smooth D ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC is very steep and terminates at the peak load. After the peak, the load drops sharply and then oscillates until the ﬁnal sharp descent. The initial incline of the ﬁbers pulled from the grout is also very steep, Table 3 Test results of matrix strength effect. internally at the base of the hook, the others ruptured at the ﬁber/ concrete interface. The ﬂattened end (F) ﬁbers embedded in VHSC ruptured at all embedment lengths except 6.35 mm. Although these ﬁbers rup- tured at 12.7 mm, they still require energy to pullout of the matrix and are worth evaluating. Pullout test results are summarized in Table 3 and illustrated in Fig. 8 . As these results show, the peak loads and pullout work were greater for the ﬁbers pulled from VHSC than the ﬁbers pulled from the grout. However, the VHSC with the lower compressive strength resulted in a higher total pull- out work. This inconsistency may be a result of the mechanics of the ﬁber ruptures. The percent increase in peak loads of VHSC over the grout specimen is 228% and 248%, and the corresponding in- crease in total pullout work is 156% and 98% respectively. Fig. 8 a shows the pullout behavior of the ﬂattened end F-ﬁber embedded in three different matrixes. The behavior of ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC was very consistent. The initial incline is linear up to the Pcrit point then increases nonlinearly to the point of rupture. In this behavior, the rupture occurs when one side of the embedded ﬂat- tened end shears off. Once the ﬁber ruptures, there is a signiﬁcant load drop, then a gradual descent until total pullout. On the other hand, the ﬁbers pulled from the grout exhibit much different behavior. The pull-out load gradually increases after the initial in- cline then drop rapidly just after the peak load. In case of the smooth F-ﬁbers, the peak loads and the total pull- out work for the VHSC specimens were much higher than the cor- responding values of the standard grout. The peak load of the smooth F-ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC is 262% greater, and the total pullout work is 425% greater than the peak load and pullout work of the ﬁbers pulled from the grout. Both the displacement at the peak and the pullout work at the peak are signiﬁcantly greater for the ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC. As shown in Fig. 8b, the pullout behaviors of the smooth F-ﬁbers are signiﬁcantly different depend- ing on which matrix they are pulled from. The initial incline of both are similar with linear branches followed by nonlinear branches ending at the peak load. The post-peak behavior of the ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC exhibit a gradual but relatively consistent decline followed by a steeper, almost linear decline to the point of com- plete pullout. The post-peak behavior of the ﬁber/grout exhibit a sharp load drop after which the load is maintained throughout most of the pullout until a gradual ﬁnal descent. The comparison between the two F-Fibers show that the VHSC ﬂattened end ﬁbers’ peak load is approximately 1.9 times that of the smooth ﬁbers. In the contrary, the pullout work is 23% less than the smooth ﬁbers because of the ﬂattened end ﬁber ruptures in the VHSC. As aforementioned, all of the F-ﬁbers that were pulled from the VHSC with an embedment depth of 12.7 mm or greater inter- nally broke at the embedded ﬂattened end. Fig. 9a graphically illus- trates the internal hook break in a VHSC specimen. Also, it was observed that the embedded ﬂattened end of the F-ﬁbers that were pulled out of the grout were signiﬁcantly worn down, almost matching the diameter of the body of the ﬁber ( Fig. 9b). The signif- icant load drop at an approximate displacement of 5 mm indicates that the ﬂattened end was worn down by that point. The total Fig. 11. Matrix effect on Polytorx P-ﬁbers em

displacement was well short of the 12.7 mm embedment length which indicates signiﬁcant tunnel damage. Further, the grout smooth F-ﬁber pullout behaved quite different from the VHSC smooth ﬁber pullout. The grout ﬁber behaved very consistently after clearly debonding, while the VHSC ﬁber appeared to increase load after debonding. Fig. 10a and b illustrates the ﬂattened end before and after pullout from a grout specimen. Fig. 10c illustrates ﬂattened end ﬁber rupture caused by pullout from a VHSC specimen. The Polytorx (P) ﬁbers embedded in VHSC ruptured at all embedment lengths. However, the peak loads for the ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC are higher than those pulled from the standard grout but due to ﬁber rupture, the pullout work in the grout is sig- niﬁcantly higher. The compressive strength of the VHSC is 263% greater than the strength of the grout, but the peak load is only 25% greater. The pullout work of the ﬁbers pulled from the grout is 579% greater than the corresponding value in the VHSC speci- mens. Fig. 11 illustrates the pullout behavior of the P-ﬁber embed- ded in VHSC and standard grout. The initial incline of the VHSC curve is linear to the point of failure. The initial incline of the ﬁber pulled from the grout is linear for approximately the ﬁrst two thirds of the incline and nonlinear for the remaining one third. The ﬁnal descent is consistent with the gradual slope of the post- peak branch of the curve. In summary, Fig. 12 shows the load–slip curves for three differ- ent cases: smooth ﬁbers such as Dramix (DS), ZP 305 (ZS), and ﬂat- tened end (FS) pulled out from standard grout are shown in Fig. 12 a; smooth ﬁbers pulled out from VHSC are shown in Fig. 12 b, and pullout behavior of the three deformed ﬁbers pulled out from VHSC is shown in Fig. 12c. Results indicate that the initial slope of the smooth ﬂat end (FS) ﬁber is slightly steeper than the ZS and DS ﬁbers. This may be due to the signiﬁcantly larger contact area caused by the larger diameter of the FS ﬁber. The initial slope of the smooth Z, D, and F-ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC are respec- tively 13%, 29%, and 35% steeper than the slopes of the ﬁbers pulled from the grout. The initial slopes of the deformed ﬁbers are also steeper when pulled from VHSC. The corresponding values are respectively 148%, 32%, and 248%. In the post-peak region, the smooth ZP 305 (ZS) and the smooth ﬂat end (FS) ﬁbers that were pulled out of the grout ( Fig. 12a) have a distinguishable drop after the peak. This indicates the bond between the grout and the ﬁber offers substantial resistance to pullout compared to the frictional resistance that controls after complete debonding. Further, it was noticed that all the post-peak portions of the smooth ﬁber curves pulled from the grout exhibit an increase in load at various points of displacement. This inconsistent behavior may be caused by damage or decay of the tunnel formed by the debonded ﬁber and the constantly decreasing embedment length. On the other hand, the post-peak behavior of the smooth ﬁbers pulled from the VHSC is more consistent than that of the grout, and there are no signiﬁ- cant load increases after the initial incline. The post-peak behavior of the ﬂattened end ﬁber pulled out from VHSC ( Fig. 12 c) shows that the internal ﬂattened end broke, hence the sharp drop in load. Fig. 13 illustrates the inﬂuence of matrix strength on the pullout behavior of the hooked and smooth ZP 305 ﬁbers pulled out from different mediums. As sho

but after the Pcrit point, the load continues to gradually increase until the ﬁnal sharp descent. Moreover, the results for the 9.5 mm embedment length indicated that the ﬁbers pulled from the lower strength VHSC, 157.6 MPa out performed the higher strength VHSC, 196 MPa in every category. There is a 24.3% differ- ence in compressive strength between the two matrixes, but the peak load of the ﬁbers pulled from lower strength matrix is 38% higher, and the total pullout work is 33.8% higher than that of the ﬁbers pulled from the higher strength matrix. Both the dis- placement at the peak and pullout work at the peak is greater for the ﬁbers pulled from the lower strength matrix. Fig. 3 shows the ﬁber pullout behavior of the two different VHSC matrix strengths. The VHSC hooked D ﬁbers’ peak load is approximately 2.5 times that of the smooth ﬁbers ( Fig. 4 ). The pullout work of the hooked ﬁber is also approximately 2.5 times the smooth ﬁber. Both the smooth and hooked ﬁbers pulled out with similar displacement indicating no major differences regarding the tunnel damage. Fur- ther, the pullout process indicated that the hooked Dramix ﬁbers traveled completely through the tunnel. This can be seen by exam- ining the pullout curve line which is fairly smooth indicating that the ﬁber did not rip or tear the matrix while bending around the curves of the tunnel ( Fig. 5). It should be noted, however, that very few type Dramix ﬁbers ruptured during the test. Table 3, also summarizes four different series of hooked Dramix ZP 305 (Z) ﬁber pullout tests with different matrix compressive strengths. The peak loads and the total pullout work for the hooked Z ﬁbers increase as the matrix compressive strengths increase. The percent increase in the peak load in ascending order is 19.5%, Fig. 4. (1 in. = 25.4 mm; 1 N = 0.225 lb; 1 MPa = 145 psi). 42 T. Abu-Lebdeh et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 39–46

50.7%, and 58%, while, the corresponding increase in the total pull- out work is 24.2%, 57.6%, and 62.4%. The pullout behavior for the hooked Z ﬁbers is illustrated in Fig. 6 a. The initial incline of both grout pullout curves exhibits nonlinear behavior before the peak load is reached, while both VHSC pullout curves exhibits linear behavior. The grout specimens exhibit less oscillatory post-peak behavior than the VHSC ﬁbers. For the smooth ZP 305 ﬁbers, both peak loads and total pullout work increase with higher matrix strengths. In comparison with the normal concrete matrix, the percent increase in very-high strength concretes’ peak loads is 105.6% and 91.5%, while the per- cent increase in total pullout work is 186.7% and 94.5% respec- tively. The pullout behavior for the smooth ZP 305 ﬁbers pulled from matrixes of varying strengths is illustrated in Fig. 6b. The ini- tial incline of the grout pullout curve exhibits linear behavior until the peak load is reached followed by steep load drop then a level- ing off affect followed by a gradual ﬁnal descent. The initial incline of both VHSC pullout curves exhibit nonlinear behavior followed by a minor, but steep load drop then a gradual load increase until the peak load is reached followed by a steep ﬁnal descent. This behavior is quite different than that of the hooked ﬁbers. Compar- ing the two ZP 305 ﬁbers embedded in VHSC ( Fig. 7 ), the hooked ﬁbers’ peak load is 1.9 times that of the smooth ﬁbers and the pull- out work of the hooked ﬁbers is 32% greater than the smooth ﬁ- bers.
